summary:
Elizabeth I's reign (1558–1603 AD) was the defining era of early modern England — the 'Elizabethan Golden Age' that produced Shakespeare's plays, defeated the [[event:2339|Spanish Armada]], witnessed the beginnings of English overseas expansion under [[event:2397|Francis Drake]] and Walter Raleigh, and established a moderate Protestant settlement that gave England relative religious stability after the violent reversals of her father Henry VIII, brother Edward VI, and sister Mary I. Elizabeth, the daughter of Anne Boleyn and thus illegitimate in Catholic eyes, skillfully navigated between Catholic and Puritan extremes with a religious settlement that was deliberately ambiguous — sufficiently Protestant to satisfy most reformers but retaining enough traditional ceremony to minimize alienation of conservatives.

Elizabeth's political genius lay in her management of the inherent vulnerability of female monarchy in a patriarchal era. She used the prospect of marriage as a diplomatic tool for decades without ever marrying, cultivated an elaborate cult of the 'Virgin Queen' that sublimated her sexuality into a quasi-religious mystique, and balanced competing noble factions through calculated favoritism — Essex, Leicester, Burghley, and Walsingham all rose and fell in her favor. Her execution of [[event:2500|Mary Queen of Scots]] in 1587 and the defeat of Philip II's Armada in 1588 were the twin peaks of her reign's security challenges. The Elizabethan era also saw the foundation of the [[event:2358|British East India Company]] (1600) and early colonization attempts in Virginia — the first shoots of what would become the [[event:105|British Empire]]. She died without an heir in 1603, ending the [[event:2470|Tudor dynasty]] and passing the throne to the Stuart James VI of Scotland.
